description: This figure presents aging curves of austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels established through hardness measurements and tensile tests, illustrating the precipitation hardening kinetics and mechanical property evolution during isothermal aging. Subfigure (a) displays Brinell hardness versus aging time (0.1–1000 h) for Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C-0.35Si steel at 450°C, 500°C, 550°C, and 600°C, demonstrating that higher aging temperatures accelerate the hardening kinetics but reduce the peak hardness due to earlier overaging. Subfigure (b) compares aging curves of six different alloys aged at 550°C—including Fe-30Mn-7.8Al-1.3C, Fe-34Mn-11Al-0.98C, Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C, Fe-31Mn-7.8Al-0.88C, Fe-29Mn-4.9Al-0.98C, and Fe-34Mn-7.1Al-0.5C—showing that increased Al and C contents enhance the age hardening effect and increase peak hardness. Subfigure (c) plots tensile properties versus aging time for Fe-30Mn-8.5Al-2.0C (550°C), Fe-28Mn-9Al-1.8C (450°C), and Fe-30Mn-7.8Al-1.3C (550°C), where UTS and YS reach maxima at intermediate aging times while total elongation (TE) progressively decreases. The figure establishes that κ'-carbide precipitation provides the strengthening mechanism, with peak properties requiring substantial aging times (10–100 h), and highlights the trade-off between strength and ductility controlled by alloy chemistry and thermal history.
